course.title: Complete Python Developer Bootcamp
course.short_description: Master Python from beginner to professional in 8 weeks with hands-on projects
course.description:
Transform into a job-ready Python developer with hands-on projects and real-world applications.

✅ What You'll Learn:
- Python fundamentals to advanced
- Web development with Django
- RESTful API development
- Database design & integration
- Deployment & DevOps basics

👥 Who This Is For:
- Career changers new to programming
- Developers learning Python
- Anyone building a tech career

🎓 What You'll Build:
- 5 portfolio projects
- Complete e-commerce application
- REST API service
- Deployment to production

- No programming experience required
- Computer with 8GB RAM minimum
- Commitment: 10-15 hours/week

✓ Write professional Python code
✓ Build web applications with Django
✓ Create and consume RESTful APIs
✓ Deploy applications to production
✓ Complete 5 portfolio projects
✓ Pass technical interviews
course.target_audience: career changers, aspiring developers, professionals seeking automation skills

modules.title: Module 1: Python Fundamentals
modules.description: Master the basics - syntax, data types, control flow, and functions
learning_objectives: Work with variables, data types, and operators
Use conditional statements and loops effectively
Write and call functions with parameters

lessons.title: Understanding Python Variables and Data Types
lessons.description: Learn to work with variables, strings, numbers, and booleans
lessons.content: In this lesson, you'll learn the foundation of Python programming - how to store and manipulate data using variables.

sections.title: What Are Variables?
sections.text_content:
# Variables in Python

Variables are containers for storing data values. In Python, you don't need to declare the type explicitly.

## Key Points:
- Use descriptive names
- Variables are case sensitive
- Cannot start with numbers
- Use snake_case naming convention

sections.title: Variable Declaration Demo
sections.description: Watch as I demonstrate variable declaration and usage

sections.title: Code Example: Variable Types
sections.code_content:
# String variable
name = 'John Doe'

# Integer variable
age = 30

# Float variable
price = 99.99

# Boolean variable
is_student = True

print(f'{name} is {age} years old')
print(f'Price: ${price}')
print(f'Is student: {is_student}')

quiz_questions.text: Which is the correct way to create a string variable in Python?
quiz_questions.explanation: Python accepts both single and double quotes for strings. The syntax 'name = value' is correct.
The number 5 without a decimal point is an integer in Python.

resources.title: Python Variables Cheat Sheet
resources.description: Quick reference for Python variable types and naming conventions

resources.title: Practice Exercises - Variables
resources.text_content: Complete these exercises to reinforce your learning

lessons.title: Project: Building a Calculator App
lessons.description: Apply what you've learned by building a functional calculator
lessons.content: Build a command-line calculator that performs basic arithmetic operations. This project will help you practice functions, user input, and conditional logic.

sections.title: Demo: The Final Calculator
sections.description: See the calculator in action before you build it

sections.title: Project Requirements
sections.text_content:
# Calculator Project Requirements

## Features to Implement:
1. Addition (+)
2. Subtraction (-)
3. Multiplication (*)
4. Division (/)
5. Error handling for division by zero
6. User-friendly menu
7. Loop to allow multiple calculations

## Bonus Challenges:
- Add power (exponent) operation
- Add square root function
- Keep calculation history

modules.title: Module 2: Object-Oriented Programming
modules.description: Learn OOP concepts - classes, objects, inheritance, and polymorphism

lessons.title: Introduction to Classes and Objects
lessons.description: Understanding the concept of classes and how to create objects

sections.title: Real-World Analogy
sections.text_content:
# Classes: The Blueprint

Think of a class as a blueprint for a house. The blueprint defines:
- Number of rooms
- Size dimensions
- Features and amenities

But the blueprint itself is not a house. When you build a house from the blueprint, that's an object (instance).

## Example:
- Class: Car (blueprint)
- Objects: Your Toyota, My Honda, Their Tesla

Each car object has the same structure (4 wheels, engine, etc.) but different values (color, model, year).

sections.title: Creating Your First Class
sections.description: Watch me create a simple Car class

sections.title: Class Syntax Example
sections.code_content:
class Car:
    def __init__(self, brand, model, year):
        self.brand = brand
        self.model = model
        self.year = year
        self.speed = 0
    
    def accelerate(self, increment):
        self.speed += increment
        print(f'{self.brand} is now going {self.speed} mph')
    
    def brake(self):
        self.speed = 0
        print(f'{self.brand} has stopped')

# Creating objects
my_car = Car('Toyota', 'Camry', 2022)
your_car = Car('Honda', 'Civic', 2021)

# Using methods
my_car.accelerate(30)
my_car.accelerate(20)
my_car.brake()
